skill.name: linux-shell-scripting
skill.description: Create and review Bash script templates for Linux backups, monitoring, user management, log analysis, networking, automation, and file operations.
skill.summary: Linux Bash scripting templates for common system administration and automation tasks.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis correctly detected many shell, filesystem, network, sudo, cron, and remote execution patterns. Manual review found these are Bash administration templates, not a confirmed malicious skill, but the templates can modify systems, delete data, and collect host information. Publish only with clear warnings and user confirmation expectations.

medium_findings.title: Privileged system modification templates
medium_findings.description: The skill includes package installation and service control examples that use sudo. This is legitimate for Linux administration, but it can alter host state and requires explicit operator review.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines clearly show sudo package installation and service start or restart commands. Context indicates administrative templates, not confirmed malicious intent.

medium_findings.title: Scheduled and background execution examples
medium_findings.description: The cron and nohup examples can create persistent or long-running execution. They are documented as scheduling patterns, but the same patterns can be misused.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The static persistence findings are semantically present in cron and nohup examples. The surrounding text presents quick-reference usage rather than stealth behavior.

medium_findings.title: Destructive file operation templates
medium_findings.description: Several examples remove files or synchronize with deletion enabled. These templates can cause data loss if paths are wrong or variables are not reviewed.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands directly delete backups, remove old files, and run rsync with delete semantics. The risk is operational rather than evidence of malware.

medium_findings.title: Remote and network execution capability
medium_findings.description: The skill includes ping, curl, interface inspection, and SSH remote script execution examples. These are common administration tasks but expand network and execution reach.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Network commands and SSH execution are explicit in the examples. The targets are placeholders or public uptime checks, so malicious exfiltration is not confirmed.

low_findings.title: System and account reconnaissance examples
low_findings.description: The monitoring examples read system state, account names, host details, and process data. This information can be sensitive on shared or production systems.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands clearly collect local system and user information. The context is health reporting and password expiry checks, so the risk is disclosure rather than proven abuse.

low_findings.title: Weak cryptography detections appear to be false positives
low_findings.description: The static weak-cryptography findings do not match a confirmed weak algorithm in the reviewed security examples. The encryption template uses AES-256-CBC with PBKDF2.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found AES-256-CBC with PBKDF2 in the encryption example and no evidence of MD5, DES, or RC4 use. CBC mode still needs careful handling, but the static weak-crypto labels are not confirmed.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell commands that change system state
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill provides command templates for installation, service control, cron scheduling, deletion, synchronization, and SSH execution. These require confirmation before use.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are concrete and high-impact, but they are presented as administrative examples. This supports a warning-level assessment instead of a malicious-content block.

content.value_statement: Linux teams need repeatable scripts for routine operations. This skill provides Bash templates for backups, monitoring, logs, networking, and automation.
actual_capabilities: Draft Bash scripts for directory, remote, database, and rotating backups.
Create monitoring scripts for CPU, disk, uptime, memory, processes, and system health.
Generate user management examples for account creation and password expiry reporting.
Build log analysis scripts for syslog and web server access logs.
Provide network checks for hosts, websites, interfaces, routes, and remote script execution.
Create automation templates for packages, cron tasks, services, file cleanup, and Git updates.
limitations: Generated scripts require review before use on production systems.
Many examples assume Linux utilities such as bash, rsync, openssl, curl, systemctl, and cron.
Some tasks require sudo or root permissions and can change host state.
Templates use placeholders that must be replaced with environment-specific paths and names.

use_cases.title: Create backup automation
use_cases.description: Generate Bash scripts for local archives, remote rsync copies, database dumps, and backup rotation.

use_cases.title: Monitor server health
use_cases.description: Create scripts that report CPU, disk, memory, uptime, processes, logs, and network connectivity.

use_cases.title: Standardize operations scripts
use_cases.description: Draft reusable shell templates for package installs, services, cron jobs, cleanup, and repository updates.

prompt_templates.title: Create a simple backup script
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Bash script that archives one directory into a timestamped backup file. Include variables for source and destination paths.

prompt_templates.title: Add server health checks
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Linux health check script that records uptime, memory, disk usage, CPU load, and top processes into one report file.

prompt_templates.title: Build safe cleanup automation
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a cleanup script for files older than a configurable number of days. Add a dry-run mode and path validation.

prompt_templates.title: Review production shell automation
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this Bash automation plan for safety, logging, error handling, permissions, destructive commands, and rollback steps.

output_examples.input: Request a backup rotation script for a Linux server.
output_examples.output: A Bash script outline with configurable backup path, retention count, timestamped names, and deletion safeguards.

output_examples.input: Ask for a system health report script.
output_examples.output: A structured report plan covering uptime, load average, memory, disk space, network interfaces, and active users.

output_examples.input: Ask for a safe cleanup script.
output_examples.output: A cleanup workflow that validates the target directory, supports dry-run output, and logs removed files.

best_practices: Review every generated command before running it on a real system.
Use dry-run modes, explicit paths, and backups before destructive operations.
Run privileged scripts only with the minimum permissions required.
anti_patterns: Do not run generated sudo, cron, rm, rsync, or ssh commands without review.
Do not hardcode production secrets, passwords, tokens, or private hostnames in scripts.
Do not use placeholder paths or service names on production systems.

faq.question: What does this skill create?
faq.answer: It creates Bash script templates for common Linux administration and automation tasks.

faq.question: Can it run scripts for me?
faq.answer: No. It provides script guidance and templates. Users must choose when and where to execute them.

faq.question: Is it safe for production use?
faq.answer: Use it with review. Some templates can modify services, schedules, packages, files, and remote hosts.

faq.question: Which tools does it support?
faq.answer: The marketplace metadata lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: Does it require root access?
faq.answer: Some examples require sudo or root permissions. Many monitoring and reporting scripts can run as a regular user.

faq.question: What should I customize first?
faq.answer: Replace placeholder paths, hosts, service names, retention values, and notification targets before use.
